coredata.dc:title: Comprehensive assessment of some l-amino acids as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl: Insights from experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Pour-Ali, Sadegh
Tavangar, Reza
Hejazi, Seyedsina
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the corrosion inhibition performance of l-asparagine (L-Asp), l-isoleucine (L-Iso), and l-proline (L-Pro) amino acids as eco-friendly compounds was investigated on mild steel coupons immersed in 0.5 M HCl solution. To this end, gravimetric analysis, electrochemical techniques, field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and quantum chemical/molecular dynamics simulations were employed. At 1000 ppm, L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro show the inhibition efficiencies of around 95%, 93%, and 91%, respectively. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm holds well for all three inhibitors, and based on the thermodynamic data, the adsorption of these inhibitors to the steel surface is involved both physisorption and chemisorption. As evidenced by the XPS results, the donor-acceptor interactions between the heteroatoms (namely, N) and surface Fe atoms play a pivotal role in the chemisorption. In line with the experimental findings, theoretical simulations unveiled potential binding sites accountable for the molecular adsorption on the surface of the steel, thereby providing insights into the underlying mechanism of corrosion inhibition.

head.ce:title: Comprehensive assessment of some l-amino acids as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl: Insights from experimental and theoretical studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the corrosion inhibition performance of l-asparagine (L-Asp), l-isoleucine (L-Iso), and l-proline (L-Pro) amino acids as eco-friendly compounds was investigated on mild steel coupons immersed in 0.5 M HCl solution. To this end, gravimetric analysis, electrochemical techniques, field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and quantum chemical/molecular dynamics simulations were employed. At 1000 ppm, L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro show the inhibition efficiencies of around 95%, 93%, and 91%, respectively. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm holds well for all three inhibitors, and based on the thermodynamic data, the adsorption of these inhibitors to the steel surface is involved both physisorption and chemisorption. As evidenced by the XPS results, the donor-acceptor interactions between the heteroatoms (namely, N) and surface Fe atoms play a pivotal role in the chemisorption. In line with the experimental findings, theoretical simulations unveiled potential binding sites accountable for the molecular adsorption on the surface of the steel, thereby providing insights into the underlying mechanism of corrosion inhibition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent strength, high toughness, good weldability, and improved manufacturability, mild steels are employed to construct a broad range of components used in the civil, military, transportation, marine, power plant, oil, gas and petrochemical industries. However, these materials are very vulnerable to corrosion in acidic environments which leads to several direct and indirect costs [1–5]. Among different strategies for the corrosion control of mild steels in acidic conditions, using corrosion inhibitors is an easily operated, high-cost performance, and very efficient method [6,7]. Typically, the corrosion inhibitors contain heteroatoms (such as N, S and O) and organic compounds (such as nitrate and chromate) which cause their physical or chemical adsorption to the steel surface [8]. Consequently, a physisorbed or chemisorbed film forms on the surface and mitigates the corrosion rate of the steel. So far, many corrosion inhibitors have been introduced for the corrosion mitigation of mild steel in acidic environments. Due to the toxicity and environmental issues of conventional corrosion inhibitors, the development of eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors such as amino acids [9,10], oil and plant extracts [11], drugs [12] and biopolymers [13] have become appealing.

Amino acids are environmentally friendly biomolecules that can coordinate with metals through an amino group and a carboxyl group existing in their structure, and consequently act as a corrosion inhibitors. Unique features such as non-toxicity, high solubility, commercial and natural availability, high purity, and minimized adverse effects make amino acids ideal alternatives to conventional toxic corrosion inhibitors [14–17]. Ashassi-Sorkhabi and et al. [18] studied the inhibition efficiency (IE) of some amino acids on the corrosion of mild steel in HCl. Based on the formation of a physically formed film, they reported that leucine, alanine and glycine possess IE values of 91.64%, 80.22% and 78.95%, respectively. Fu et al. [19] compared the corrosion inhibition performance of tryptophan, serine, histidine and cysteine. Relying on the frontier molecular orbitals and electrochemical measurements, the IE values followed the sequence: tryptophan (96.3%) > histidine (93.4%) > cysteine (85.1%) > serine (41.5%) at a concentration of 0.01 mol/L. Li et al. [20] claimed that 100 ppm of Lentinan decreases the corrosion rate of Q235 mild steel in HCl solution by around 15 times. In an investigation conducted by Oubaaqa et al. [8], the corrosion inhibition performance of phenylalanine and aspartic acid on mild steel in a 0.5 M HCl solution was examined. At a concentration of 0.001 mol/L, the reported IE values were 87% for phenylalanine and 89% for aspartic acid. Zhang et al. [21] also reported that konjac glucomannan modified with l-arginine exhibits an IE value of around 92% at a concentration of 2000 ppm for mild steel in 0.5 HCl. Inhibition performance of methionine, lysine, threonine and valine l-amino acids has also been investigated for mild steel in acidic conditions [10,22,23]. The quest for new amino acids as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel is necessary and important due to the potential for enhanced corrosion protection, environmental considerations, cost-effectiveness, mechanistic insights, and the diversification of inhibitor options.

The current work aims to study and compare the corrosion inhibition performance of l-proline (L-Pro), l-isoleucine (L-Iso), and l-asparagine (L-Asp) amino acids for mild steel in 0.5 M HCl. To the best of our knowledge, there is a limited number of studies investigating the corrosion inhibition performance of these l-amino acids, on mild steel in HCl. While some research exists on general corrosion inhibitors, the specific evaluation of these molecules and their mechanism of action is lacking in the current literature. To fill this gap, weight loss measurements and electrochemical examinations were used to assess the IE of each compound. Moreover, the adsorption isotherms were determined to study the mechanism of adsorption. The surface of mild steel samples immersed in uninhibited (0.5 M HCl) and inhibited (0.5 M HCl + optimum concentration of corrosion inhibitor) solutions was examined through field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). Finally, Quantum chemical and molecular dynamic (MD) simulations were performed to evaluate the possible interactions between each amino acid molecule and steel surface, and validate the experimental results.

Mild steel coupons with the size of 12 mm × 12 mm × 3 mm and the chemical composition (wt.%) of 0.17 C, 0.82 Si, 0.65 Mn, 0.03 S, 0.03 P, and Fe as balance were used as the test material. The coupons were ground to 2000-grit SiC paper, washed with water and ethanol, and immediately dried with a hair dryer (cold wind) to dry the remained ethanol from the coupon. l-asparagine (L-Asp), l-proline (L-Pro), and l-isoleucine (L-Iso) amino acids were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich with no further purification. These amino acids were employed as corrosion inhibitors in the corrosion examinations. To prepare the corrosion media, hydrochloric acid (HCl, Merck 37%) was diluted with double distilled water.

The weight loss measurements were conducted following the guidelines outlined in ASTM G1-03 (2017). The weight of each coupon was first measured by an analytical balance with an accuracy of 10−5 g (Mettler Toledo XP2U). Subsequently, they were individually immersed in various test solutions containing different amounts of each corrosion inhibitor (0, 250, 500, 750, and 1000 ppm). After 24 h of immersion, the coupons were removed from the solutions, washed with running water, rinsed in distilled water, dried, and re-weighted. This considerable immersion time helps to obtain weight losses with meaningful distinction. Three identical specimens were considered for each test solution and mean values were reported. By using the following formula, inhibition efficiency (IE G) and surface coverage (θ G) were determined:

G refers to the weight loss method. W 0 and w 1 are the weight loss values of coupons in the solutions without and with corrosion inhibitors, respectively. The corrosion rate (milli-inch per year, mpy) was also calculated via the following formula:
(3)

where W, A, T and D are the weight loss (mg), the exposed area (in2), immersion time in the test solution (h), and density of mild steel coupon (g/cm3), respectively.

All electrochemical corrosion examinations were performed using an electrochemical workstation (IM6x Zahner potentiostat, Germany) with a conventional three-electrode cell (KPSCell Kit, Iran). In this cell, the mild steel coupon with an available surface of around 1 cm2 was the working electrode (WE), a platinum plate with a surface of 1 cm2 was employed as the auxiliary electrode (AE), and Ag/AgCl was the reference electrode (RE). Similar to the weight loss measurements, the electrochemical tests were done in 0.5 M HCl solutions containing various concentrations of each corrosion inhibitor (0, 250, 500, 750, and 1000 ppm). The tests included the monitoring of open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) analysis, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). For each PDP or EIS test, OCP was first measured for 1 h to reach stable conditions at the coupon/solution interface. PDP experiments were performed by scanning the potential from −0.3 to +0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl respect to the OCP with a scan rate of 1 mV/s at room temperature (∼30 °C). Different kinetics parameters including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathodic Tafel slope (β c), and anodic Tafel slope (β a) were determined by the Tafel extrapolation method, as suggested by McCafferty [24]. IE P and θ P (surface coverage) values were also obtained by the following formulas:

where subscript P refers to the PDP technique. For the efficient concentration of each corrosion inhibitor, PDP measurements were also performed at 40, 50, and 60 °C to find their adsorption mechanism. EIS was carried out in the frequency range of 105 to 10−2 Hz by applying a sinusoidal voltage with amplitude of 5 mV at OCP. The EIS data were fitted by ZView software. All electrochemical examinations were repeated at least three times to control the data reproducibility.

The surface morphology of mild steel coupons after 24 h immersion in 0.5 M HCl solutions without and with an optimized concentration of each corrosion inhibitor at room temperature (∼30 °C) was examined using FE-SEM (MIRA3 TESCAN). Moreover, the chemistry of corrosion products formed on the coupons immersed in the solutions containing an optimized concentration of each corrosion inhibitor was determined by XPS (PHI 5000 VersaProbe, Ulvac-PHI) examination. The high-resolution spectra of O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s were obtained. To obtain the binding energy regarding each peak, CASA XPS software was employed with Gaussian-Lorentzian lines and Shirley background type. The energy scale calibration was performed by placing C 1s peak at 284.82 eV.

Quantum chemical calculations and MD simulations were done via Material Studio 5.0 software. DMol3 modulus with the basis set of double numerical plus d-functions (DND) was considered for geometry optimization of different molecules and calculation of quantum chemical parameters including the energy of highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO), the energy of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E LUMO), the energy gap (i.e., ΔE = E LUMO-E HOMO), absolute electronegativity (χ), hardness (η), the fraction of transferred electrons (ΔN), and dipole moment (μ, Debye). The values of χ, η, and ΔN were determined using the following formulae [25]:

The values of χFe and ηFe are 7 and 0 eV/mol, as suggested by Chen et al. [26]. In the MD simulations, the steady adsorption configuration and binding energy of each corrosion inhibitor molecule on the surface of Fe (1 1 0) were evaluated. All calculations were optimized by COMPASS II force field and simulation time of 500 ps with 1.0 fs time step via NVT ensemble under the temperature of 298 K. The binding energy between corrosion inhibitors and Fe (1 1 0) surface was obtained using Eq. (9) [27].

Besides, E total, E substrate, and E inhibitor are the total energy of the simulation, the energy of Fe (1 1 0) substrate together with the H2O molecules, and the energy of inhibitor, respectively.

The weight loss results of the mild steel coupons immersed in the blank and inhibited solutions containing different concentrations of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro molecules at ∼30 °C for 24 h are presented in Table 1 . As can be seen, the corrosion rate of coupons decreases as the concentration of each compound increases. At a concentration of 1000 ppm, the corrosion rate in solutions containing L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro, decreases by around 22.3, 15.4 and 11.1 times, respectively. In line with this corrosion rate reduction, the IE G values reach 95.5%, 93.5%, and 91.0% for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro compounds, respectively. These findings indicate that these molecules can be employed as efficient corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl solutions. Moreover, the IE G values nonlinearly change with the concentration of each corrosion inhibitor, implying that the number of adsorption sites significantly decreases with the concentration. In other words, upon reaching the saturation point of the adsorption (∼1000 ppm for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro), the capacity of adsorption remains nearly constant and higher concentrations of each compound are not very effective and efficient.

The OCP variations of mild steel coupons in different uninhibited and inhibited solutions are shown in Fig. 1 . The initial OCP of coupons in the blank solution is found to be around −415 mV vs. Ag/AgCl and after elapsing the time, the OCP value shifts toward more active regions and reaches a nearly constant value of −448 mV after 10 min of immersion. The coupons in various inhibited solutions exhibit similar behavior in potential at the early stage of immersion and after that they show a drop so that the drop reduces as the concentration of each corrosion inhibitor increases. Subsequently, the OCP of these coupons shifts toward anodic regions and stabilizes at values that increase with the corrosion inhibitor concentration. Since the addition of all three inhibitors (L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro) shifts the potential toward more noble values, it can be claimed that these amino acids are anodic corrosion inhibitors that predominantly block the active anodic sites [28,29]. As seen from Fig. 1, L-Asp causes the most considerable anodic shift in the potential so that by the addition of 1000 ppm L-Asp, its OCP reaches an approximate value of −394 mV vs. Ag/AgCl after 45 min. The OCP of different samples possess a nearly constant value after 50 min immersion. Therefore, PDP and EIS experiments were performed after 60 min of immersion to guarantee the steady-state conditions at the coupon/solution interface.

Fig. 2 represents the PDP curves of coupons immersed in the blank and various inhibited solutions at ∼30 °C. The general overview of PDP curves shows that by adding each corrosion inhibitor and increasing its concentration, the cathodic and anodic branches shift toward lower current densities indicating the retarding the corrosion reactions occurring at the coupon/solution interface. Moreover, corresponding PDP parameters including i corr, E corr, β a, β c, IE P, and θ P are listed in Table 2 . Assessing Table 2 reveals a remarkable decrease in the corrosion current density of mild steel coupons with the increasing corrosion inhibitor concentration. This reduction can be directly ascribed to the adsorption of corrosion inhibitor molecules on the coupon surface and the blockage of active sites [30,31]. As a result, at 1000 ppm concentration, L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro yield high efficiencies of 95.2%, 93.4%, and 91.1%, respectively. Furthermore, E corr values in Table 2 reveal a considerable shift toward more anodic values after adding corrosion inhibitors. Typically, if a corrosion inhibitor changes the E corr by at least 85 mV in the cathodic direction, it is a cathodic inhibitor, and if it shifts the E corr values by at least 85 mV in the anodic direction, it is an anodic inhibitor. In this study, maximum shifting in E corr is more than +85 mV while the inhibitors are added at a concentration of 1000 ppm. So, it can be claimed that L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro act as anodic corrosion inhibitors and mainly control metal dissolution. Another point that should be considered is the reduction of Tafel slopes in the presence of corrosion inhibitors. As reported by Marcus [32], this reduction means that the corrosion processes are facilitated as the corrosion inhibitor adsorbed to the mild steel surface. In other words, by absorbing the corrosion inhibitor and sharing its electrons with the iron atoms, the activation energy barrier for dissolution of iron atoms decreases. The results of the PDP tests indicate that L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors reduce the corrosion rate of mild steel coupons with a similar mechanism; however, their inhibition performance decreases in the order of L-Asp > L-Iso > L-Pro under the same conditions.

The effect of temperature on the corrosion performance of mild steel coupons was assessed in the uninhibited and inhibited solutions containing 1000 ppm of corrosion inhibitor (most efficient concentration). The corresponding parameters extracted from the PDP measurements are also presented in Table 3 . In the same scenario for all three inhibitors, the IE P values reduce as the temperature increases from 30 °C to 60 °C so that IE P decreases by around 30%, 34%, and 50% for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors. The decomposition of these amino acids and their desorption from the steel surface can be the primary mechanisms for this observation [33,34]. To find the activation energy of mild steel dissolution in the blank and inhibited solutions (E a), the Arrhenius equation was employed (Eq. (10)) [35]:
(10)

where T, A, and R are the absolute temperature (K), frequency factor, and universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol⋅K), respectively. The values of E a can be calculated by measuring the slope of ln (i corr) vs. 1/T plot, as shown in Fig. 3 . The E a values for the blank and inhibited solutions containing L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro compounds are ∼69 kJ/mol, ∼129 kJ/mol, ∼125 kJ/mol, and ∼123 kJ/mol, respectively. This significant increase in the E a values can be attributed to the adsorbed inhibitor molecules which disturb the dissolution of iron atoms [36].

Thermodynamic activation parameters, namely the enthalpy change (ΔH*) and entropy change (ΔS*), associated with the corrosion of mild steel in both uninhibited and inhibited 0.5 M HCl solutions were calculated using the transition state formula (Eq. 11).

where h is the Planck's constant and N is the Avogadro number. Table 4 presents the values of ΔH* and ΔS* for different samples. The positive values observed for ΔH* can be attributed to the endothermic nature of the steel dissolution process. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the ΔH* value in the presence of optimized concentrations of each amino acid (i.e., 1000 ppm) is significantly higher compared to the uninhibited solution. This finding suggests that the active sites within each amino acid molecule exhibit strong adsorption onto the mild steel surface. In these conditions, ΔS* for the inhibited solutions (namely, for the Asp-containing solution) shows an elevation, indicating an increase in disorder during the transformation of reactants into the activated complex [7,31].

According to Eq. (12), inhibitor molecules should first push away the pre-adsorbed water molecules to be adsorbed on the surface of mild steel coupon:
(12)

where H2O(ads), H2O(soln), Org (ads), and Org (soln) are adsorbed water molecules, bulk water molecules, adsorbed inhibitors, and dissolved inhibitors, respectively. Besides, n is the mole ratio, i.e., the number of water molecules substituted by one inhibitor molecule. The adsorption isotherm determines how the surface coverage (θ) depends on the inhibitor concentration. As shown in Fig. 4 , different adsorption isotherms including Langmuir, Frumkin, Temkin, and Flory-Huggins were adopted to investigate the adsorption behavior of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors [20,37,38]. Based on the results of data fitting (Fig. 4), Langmuir adsorption isotherm is found to provide an ideal fit, as compared with the other isotherms. According to Langmuir isotherm:
(13)

where C and K ads are the inhibitor concentration and the adsorption equilibrium constant. K ads is obtained from the intercept of C/θ vs. θ plot, and employed to calculate ΔG°ads (standard adsorption free energy) [39,40]:
(14)

where 106 is the mass concentration of water. The mean value of ΔG°ads for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro containing solutions is −36.578 kJ/mol, −37.583 kJ/mol, and −38.371 kJ/mol, respectively. In general, physisorption, chemisorption, and physiochemisorption are ascribed to ΔG°ads > −20 kJ/mol, ΔG°ads < −40 kJ/mol, and −40 kJ/mol <ΔG°ads < −20 kJ/mol, respectively [41,42]. So, the adsorption of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro corrosion inhibitors is most likely to be physiochemisorption, i.e., two types of bonding can be considered: (i) electrostatic adsorption of inhibitor molecules and metallic atoms, and (ii) the formation of coordination bonds between lone electron pairs of heteroatoms (namely, N and O) of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro molecules and the vacant d-orbits on the surface of steel coupons.

EIS is a well-established analysis which is extensively employed to assess the kinetics of corrosion processes. Nyquist, Bode-modulus, and Bode-phase plots of mild steel coupons immersed in the solutions without and with different concentrations of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro at 30 °C are displayed in Fig. 5 . Nyquist plots of all three inhibitors represent one depressing capacitive semicircle, meaning that there is solely one time constant regarding the charge transfer process (Fig. 5a). Microstructural inhomogeneity and roughness existing on the surface of mild steel coupons caused frequency dispersion, and consequently, such depressed capacitive semicircles appear [43]. As the concentration of each inhibitor increases, the diameter of corresponding Nyquist plot also enlarges, implying that the surface coverage of the inhibitor and its inhibition performance enhances. Under similar conditions, L-Asp shows the largest diameters in the semicircles and possesses the highest inhibition performance, as supported by the gravimetric and PDP results. The addition of different corrosion inhibitors does not change the typical shape of Nyquist plots which in turn reveal that the added amino acids do not affect the governing corrosion mechanisms of steel coupons in 0.5 M HCl solution. In line with Nyquist plots, Bode-modulus and Bode-phase plots also reveal one time constant denoting that the inhibition performance of these amino acids is correlated with the electrical double layer existing at the mild steel coupon/solution interface [44]. As the concentration of each inhibitor increases, the amount of adsorbed inhibitor increases, the charge transfer process becomes more difficult, and consequently corresponding Bode-modulus and Bode-phase plots go up.

The EIS data were modeled by the electrical equivalent circuit represented in Fig. 6 and extracted electrochemical parameters are presented in Table 5 . The circuit consists of a parallel connection of polarization resistance (R p) and constant phase element of double layer (CPE dl) with the solution resistance (R s) which is connected in series with R p and CPE [45]. CPE is commonly used instead of a capacitor in these circuits to represent a non-ideal capacitance. The impedance of the CPE (Z CPE) can be calculated using Eq. (15) [46]:
(15)

where Q is the CPE constant, ω is the angular frequency, j is the imaginary root, and n is the diffusion effect index. The CPE represents a pure resistance and a pure capacitance when n = 0 and n = 1, respectively. The value of electrical double layer capacitance (C dl) can be determined by Eq. (16) [47]:
(16)

From Table 5, when the concentration of corrosion inhibitor reaches 1000 ppm, the R p values increase to 1311 Ω cm2, 1029 Ω cm2, and 894 Ω cm2 for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro, respectively, while this value is 65 Ω cm2 for the blank solution. Moreover, by adding any of these amino acids, C dl values continuously decrease. This decrease can be interpreted in terms of the Helmholtz model (Eq. (17)) [24]:
(17)
C
d l
=
ε
ε 0
d
S
where ε is the local dielectric constant, ε 0 is the air permittivity, d is the double layer thickness, and S is the exposed surface of the steel coupon. With increasing the concentration of these inhibitors, thickness of double layer increases, the exposed surface of coupon decreases, ε decreases (replacement of water molecules with inhibitor molecules), and as a consequence, C dl significantly decreases. The comparison of C dl values in Table 5 shows that L-Asp possesses the lowest amount of C dl under similar conditions, which are in line with the results of PDP and gravimetric tests. It is worth noting that very small values of chi-squared (χ2) reveal a good agreement between the data points and fitted results.

The surface morphologies of mild steel coupons after 24 h immersion in the blank and various inhibited solutions (0.5 M HCl + 1000 ppm of corrosion inhibitor) are depicted in Fig. 7 . As can be seen from Fig. 7a, the unexposed sample shows a uniform and smooth surface with some typical micro-scratches caused by grinding. In contrast, when this surface is exposed to a 0.5 M HCl without an inhibitor, it suffers from severe corrosion attack in such a way that its entire surface is covered by corrosion products and the surface becomes utterly rough on a microscopic scale (Fig. 7b). For the inhibited solutions, the corrosion attack is remarkably lower than the blank one. For the sample immersed in L-Asp containing media (Fig. 7c), few signs of local corrosion attacks are observed. These types of attacks have a greater contribution for the coupon immersed in the solution containing L-Iso (Fig. 7d). However, there are still signs of uncorroded surfaces on both latter mentioned samples. For the coupon in the presence L-Pro, both general and local attacks of corrosion are observed; however, the severity of the surface damage is not as great as the coupon immersed in the blank solution (Fig. 7e). The FE-SEM observations also confirm this order of inhibition efficiency: L-Asp > L-Iso > L-Pro.

Although the effectiveness of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors was demonstrated through different techniques, XPS is a pivotal examination to achieve a true perception of the surface chemistry of steel coupons in the presence of each inhibitor. The XPS test was performed on the mild steel coupons immersed in 0.5 M HCl containing 1000 ppm of corrosion inhibitor for 24 h. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s are shown in Fig. 8 . It should first mention that the simultaneous presence of O-, C-, and N-containing species for the studied corrosion inhibitors reveals their adsorption on the surface of steel coupons. As presented in Fig. 8, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro can be predominantly fitted to four peaks located at 532.2 ± 0.1, 531.2 ± 0.1, 530.5 ± 0.1, and 529.5 ± 0.1 eV which are associated with the C–O bond, CO bond, FeOOH, and Fe2O3, respectively [29,48]. A minor peak at 529.9 ± 0.1 was also identified only for L-Asp, which can be assigned to FeO [49]. The common peaks for the C 1s spectrum of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors at binding energies of 288.3 ± 0.1, 285.8 ± 0.1, and 284.6 ± 0.1 eV are attributed to O–CO, C–O, and C–C bonds, respectively [50–52]. The peak located at 288.3 ± 0.1 can also be assigned to N–CO for L-Asp. Besides, a minor peak at 287.7 ± 0.1 corresponds to the C–N bond, which was not recognized for the samples immersed in L-Iso and L-Pro containing solutions. Meanwhile, the N 1s narrow spectra of the coupons immersed in L-Asp and L-Iso containing media can be deconvoluted into three peaks located at 399.7 ± 0.1, 399.4 ± 0.1, and 398.7 ± 0.1 eV which are corresponded to N–C, N–Fe, and N–H bonds, respectively [51,53]. For the L-Pro inhibitor, the peaks regarding the N–C and N–H were recognized but the N–Fe bond was not detected. Although all three corrosion inhibitors show nearly similar surface chemistry, the main difference is in the intensity of signals related to each bond or compound. As can be deduced from the intensity of the peaks regarding identical bonds or compounds in various samples (Fig. 8), the order of the intensities is as follows: L-Asp > L-Iso > L-Pro, which is similar to the order of IE p values. This order shows that L-Asp provides highest amount of adsorption sites to the mild steel surface.

Quantum chemical calculations were carried out to investigate the influence of electronic parameters and molecular structures on the inhibition performance of studied inhibitors. Fig. 9 shows the optimized molecular structures and the distribution of front molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO). Moreover, various quantum chemical parameters including E HOMO, E LUMO, ΔE, χ, η, ΔN, and μ are presented in Table 6 . Unlike the L-Pro inhibitor, the electron clouds of both HOMO and LUMO for L-Asp and L-Iso are nearly distributed at O–CO and C–N bonds, revealing that these regions can be served as active sites for their adsorption to the steel surface and forming covalent bonds. The parameters in Table 5 can evaluate the inhibition performance of an inhibitor from different points of view [54–56]. ΔE reflects the stability strength of an inhibitor and its smaller values show that the compound has a higher tendency to be adsorbed on the surface. As the value of χ increases the tendency to form covalent bonds and fill iron vacant d-orbitals increases. Based on the HSAB theory, Fe atoms are soft acids and molecules with the higher softness (lower η), which have higher reactivity, can easily share electrons and make covalent bonds. As reported by Wazzan et al. [57], the electron donating capacity of corrosion inhibitor, and consequently its inhibition performance enhances when 0 <ΔN< 3.6. In the case of μ, if this value for an inhibitor is higher than the dipole moment of water (1.85 debye), it means that the inhibitor has a higher tendency to push away pre-adsorbed water molecules and adsorb on the steel surface. Given the interpretation of each quantum chemical parameter, it can be claimed that these results agree with the experimental results and prove the inhibition performance order of: L-Asp > L-Iso > L-Pro.

Fig. 10 represents the low-energy adsorption configuration of L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro corrosion inhibitors adsorbed on the surface of Fe (1 1 0). From a side view, the configuration of all three inhibitors is parallel to the surface of Fe with nearly no significant distortion. In this condition, heteroatoms, i.e., N and O, are very close to the surface. Besides, the flat orientation provides the highest contact and surface coverage, guaranteeing an ideal inhibitor/steel system interaction. The E adsorption values are −255.3, −163.2, and −125.5 kJ/mol for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro inhibitors, respectively. The adsorption energies for all three inhibitors are negative, meaning that the adsorption in spontaneous. Besides, the E binding values for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro are 255.3, 163.2, and 125.5 kJ/mol, respectively. Since L-Asp containing media possesses the highest value of E binding, it can be claimed that this system is the most stable adsorption one, as compared with the L-Iso and L-Pro systems.

As depicted in Fig. 11 , in the presence of HCl solution, corrosion reactions take place on the surface of mild steel. In this regard, H+ ions are adsorbed onto the metallic surface, leading to the dissolution of Fe atoms and formation of H2 molecules. Moreover, Cl− anions are adsorbed on the steel surface, resulting in the formation of corrosion products such as iron oxides, iron hydroxides, and iron chlorides. Specifically, the dissolution rate of mild steel in HCl is governed by the following reactions [58]:

Upon introduction of the investigated corrosion inhibitors into the acidic solution, the amino groups (-NH2) present in these molecules readily undergo protonation in the acidic environment, enabling their adsorption onto the steel surface through electrostatic interactions. Consequently, a substantial coverage of the corrosion-active region is achieved by the adsorbed corrosion inhibitor molecules, effectively impeding the corrosion process. At an optimized concentration, these corrosion inhibitor molecules can display additional attractive forces such as induction force, orientation force, and dispersion force, which promote their mutual interaction and facilitate the coverage of the entire steel surface. In the presence of Cl− ions in the acidic solution, a competitive adsorption scenario arises between the corrosion inhibitors and Cl− ions onto the steel surface. The positively charged protonated amino groups of the corrosion inhibitors and the negatively charged Cl− ions can engage in electrostatic interactions with the steel surface. However, the attractive forces mentioned earlier allow the corrosion inhibitors to effectively compete with Cl− ions for adsorption sites on the steel surface. Consequently, a substantial coverage of the steel surface by the corrosion inhibitors is achieved, mitigating the corrosive attack on the metal and providing enhanced corrosion protection.

In addition to the physisorption, the chemisorption process is primarily governed by the interaction between the lone pair electrons on the N and O heteroatoms of these l-amino acids and the vacant d-orbitals of the steel surface. The bonding involving the O heteroatoms typically leads to the formation of iron oxides and hydroxides, as evidenced by Fig. 8. On the other hand, bonding with the N heteroatom can result in the formation of N–Fe bonds, as supported by Fig. 8 for L-Asp and L-Iso. It should be noted that L-Asp contains two N atoms, which exhibit a slight distortion in the molecule, facilitating the formation of chemical bonds with the surface. Conversely, L-Iso and L-Pro have only one N atom, and in the case of L-Pro, it is constrained within a ring structure, limiting its ability to contribute significantly to bond formation with Fe atoms (Fig. 8, N 1s for L-Pro). Therefore, it is plausible to attribute the slight difference in inhibition efficiencies among these molecules at a concentration of 1000 ppm to the presence of a higher number of heteroatoms, specifically N, in the L-Asp.

The most important conclusions of the present study can be summarized in the following items:
(i)
L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro compounds can be potential corrosion inhibitors for mild steel corrosion in 0.5 M HCl.
(ii)
According to the gravimetric and PDP results, at 1000 ppm inhibitor concentration, the inhibition performance increases as follows: L-Asp (IE = ∼95%) > L-Iso (IE = ∼93%) > L-Pro (IE = ∼91%).
(iii)
The adsorption of all three inhibitors follows the Langmuir isotherm and exhibits both physisorption and chemisorption onto the surface of mild steel. XPS results reveal that the chemisorption is mainly attributed to the donor-acceptor interactions between the heteroatoms (specifically, N) and Fe atoms.
(iv)
The MD simulations show that L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro are adsorbed onto the steel surface in an almost flat configuration. Besides, the E binding values for L-Asp, L-Iso, and L-Pro are 255.3, 163.2, and 125.5 kJ/mol, respectively.
